front: Figure: served as the first US Secretary of State, under George Washington
Figure: served as the second US Minister to France, where he introduced the word "pedicure" to English
Figure: the only multi-term US president to cast zero vetoes
Figure: as a poor public speaker, began the practice of submitting written State of the Union addresses, a practice continued until 1917
Figure: his navy and war secretaries were Robert Smith and Henry Dearborn
Document: wrote the first draft of the Declaration of Independence as part of the Committee of Five, whose wording Congress extensively debated
Concept: distinguished a "natural aristocracy" of "virtue and talents" from an "artificial aristocracy" of "wealth and birth"
Concept: advocated for an agrarian America of "yeoman farmers," opposing Alexander Hamilton's centralized, pro-manufacturing vision
Document: after resigning the vice presidency to protest the Alien and Sedition Acts, Jefferson authored this set of resolutions arguing states could refuse to follow unconstitutional federal laws
Figure: described the Missouri Compromise as a "fire bell in the night" and warned that the "tree of liberty" must be refreshed with the blood of patriots and tyrants
Document: in an 1802 letter to the Danbury Baptists, this man called for a "wall of separation" between church and state
Document: author of this Virginia statute establishing religious freedom
Figure: founded the University of Virginia, in Charlottesville, out of dissatisfaction with the state's religious institutions
Work: used a razor and glue to cut and paste sections of the New Testament, selecting only "the very words of Jesus" and excising every miracle
back: The Life and Morals of Jesus of Nazareth (Thomas Jefferson)

front: Work: attempted to retell the Gospels without supernatural phenomena in a naturalized-religion text, reflecting the author's deist/unitarian views
back: The Life and Morals of Jesus of Nazareth (Thomas Jefferson)

front: Document: also wrote a plan for establishing uniformity in the coinage, weights, and measures of the United States
Work: divided into 23 "Queries" (including "Climate" and "Laws") about the author's home state
back: Notes on the State of Virginia (Thomas Jefferson)
work: Notes on the State of Virginia & Views on Race

front: Work: includes a table comparing quadruped sizes in Europe and North America to refute Comte de Buffon's theory of New World degeneracy
back: Notes on the State of Virginia (Thomas Jefferson)
work: Notes on the State of Virginia & Views on Race

front: Work: reproduces an oration by the Mingo chief Logan as part of its refutation of Buffon's degeneracy theory
back: Notes on the State of Virginia (Thomas Jefferson)
work: Notes on the State of Virginia & Views on Race

front: Figure: wrote that Black Americans had "misery enough, God knows, but no poetry," criticizing the poet Phillis Wheatley
work: Notes on the State of Virginia & Views on Race

front: Work: in its "Manufactures" section, claims "those who labor in the earth are the chosen people of God," idealizing agrarian "yeoman farmers"
back: Notes on the State of Virginia (Thomas Jefferson)
work: Notes on the State of Virginia & Views on Race

front: Figure: wrote that white women's ability to blush made them aesthetically superior to Black women
work: Notes on the State of Virginia & Views on Race

front: Figure: refused to pay tribute to the Pasha of Tripoli that his predecessor John Adams had negotiated, triggering this war
work: Presidency: Barbary Wars, Embargo & Louisiana/Lewis and Clark

front: Figure: after a decisive victory over the Barbary Pirates, chose to sign a peace treaty rather than continue the war; later signed the Embargo Act
work: Presidency: Barbary Wars, Embargo & Louisiana/Lewis and Clark

front: Event: commissioned this 1803 expedition, partly hoping it would find a living mastodon
work: Presidency: Barbary Wars, Embargo & Louisiana/Lewis and Clark

front: Figure: as a fossil collector, argued that mastodon remains disproved Buffon's view that American animals were degenerate
work: Presidency: Barbary Wars, Embargo & Louisiana/Lewis and Clark

front: Event: as president, Jefferson secured this 1803 purchase of French territory
work: Presidency: Barbary Wars, Embargo & Louisiana/Lewis and Clark

front: Figure: during impeached judge John Pickering's trial, Pickering challenged this sitting president to a duel
work: Presidency: Barbary Wars, Embargo & Louisiana/Lewis and Clark

front: Figure: collaborated with Charles-Louis Clerisseau to design the Virginia State Capitol, replicating the Maison Carree
work: Architecture: Monticello, UVA & the Virginia State Capitol

front: Building: this Jefferson-designed mansion has an octagonal, skylight-studded dome influenced by the Hotel de Salm, which he saw in Paris
work: Architecture: Monticello, UVA & the Virginia State Capitol

front: Building: this Jefferson-designed mansion's entrance hall has a great clock with an exterior face bearing only one hand
work: Architecture: Monticello, UVA & the Virginia State Capitol

front: Figure: eschewed grand staircases in favor of hidden, "suppressed" ones in many of his building designs
work: Architecture: Monticello, UVA & the Virginia State Capitol

front: Building: this Jefferson-designed retreat included extra slave quarters along its Mulberry Row
work: Architecture: Monticello, UVA & the Virginia State Capitol

front: Figure: designed the Lawn's pavilions and the central Rotunda at this university, alongside his own mansion Monticello
work: Architecture: Monticello, UVA & the Virginia State Capitol

front: Figure: journalist James Callender, after being denied a postmaster appointment, exposed this president's affair with the enslaved Sally Hemings
Figure: accused of hypocrisy for owning slaves in a letter from Benjamin Banneker, who also sent him an almanac urging recognition of Black equality
Work: this William Wells Brown novel fictionalizes a half-Black daughter of Thomas Jefferson and Sally Hemings
Figure: Ibram X. Kendi's Stamped from the Beginning titles a section for him, saying he "rested in the comfort of slavery" from earliest childhood until his "final lucid moment"
Figure: reconciled with this former rival through the mediation of Benjamin Rush, then exchanged heavily studied letters with him for fifteen years
Figure: he and John Adams died within hours of each other on July 4, 1826; one's last words claimed the other survived him
Figure: in a letter exchange with John Adams, one of the two claimed "the same political parties which now agitate the U.S. have existed through all time"
Figure: in a letter to John Adams, blamed "Christian Priests, Jewish Rabbis, Grecian Sages and Roman Emperors" for the loss of history of nations more ancient than Christ
Work: the correspondence between Jefferson and John Adams, along with letters of one of their wives, was comprehensively compiled by this editor
Work: this Joseph Ellis book discusses Jefferson and John Adams's views on race and inequality in "then" and "now" sections
Figure: coined the word "neologize" and introduced "catenary" into English, in a letter about bridge construction
Document: outlines procedures for dealing with "disorderly words"; written while its author served as Vice President
Figure: sold his personal book collection to found this institution in 1815, replacing the collection destroyed in the War of 1812
Figure: in 1802, the townspeople of Chester, Massachusetts presented him with a 1,235-pound "Mammoth Cheese" inscribed "Rebellion to Tyrants is Obedience to God"
Work: this Joseph Ellis biography argues Jefferson was a "feminine" and "dinner table" politician who hid from the public
Figure: the first comprehensive biography of him was written by early science-fiction author and Congressman George Tucker
Figure: in his conservative later years, novelist John Dos Passos wrote a study of this man's "head and heart"
